1: Isaiah 38:1 introduces the second major crisis of the Hezekiah narrative — the king's illness: 'bayyamim hahem chala Chizkiyahu lamutt, in those days Hezekiah became ill to dying.' The bayamim hahem (in those days) suggests proximity to the Assyrian crisis — the narrative sequence (Assyrian siege → Hezekiah's illness → Babylonian envoys) moves from foreign threat to personal mortality to the pivot toward the Babylonian exile. 'And Isaiah son of Amoz the prophet came to him and said: Thus says the LORD: Set your house in order, for you shall die and will not recover' (ki met attah velo tichyeh, for you are dying and will not live). The initial oracle is death, not life — YHWH's first word is the sentence before the reversal. The 'set your house in order' (tsav leveitekha, give orders to your household = prepare your legal affairs, designate successors, arrange debts and inheritances) is the practical instruction before death.
2: 'Then Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the LORD' — the physical posture of prayer: 'vayassov Chizkiyahu panav el-haqqir, and Hezekiah turned his face to the wall.' The turning of the face to the wall: (1) a posture of privacy — prayer away from the officials and visitors in the sickroom; (2) a posture of turning away from the world of the living toward the wall = the threshold of death; (3) a posture of turning toward YHWH — since the wall faced toward the temple in Israelite sacred geography, turning to the wall was turning toward the divine presence. The same root palal (to pray) as in Isaiah 37:15's 'Hezekiah prayed about Sennacherib' — the same king uses the same prayer-posture for both the national crisis and the personal crisis.
3: 'He prayed: Please, LORD, remember how I have walked before you in faithfulness and with a whole heart, and have done what is good in your sight. And Hezekiah wept bitterly' — Hezekiah's prayer contains two claims: (1) 'asher hithalakhti lefanekhah be'emet uvlev shalem, that I have walked before you in faithfulness and with a whole heart.' The 'walking before YHWH in faithfulness' (emeth, covenant-faithfulness) and 'whole heart' (lev shalem = undivided heart of complete covenant loyalty, cf. 1 Kings 11:4: Solomon's heart was not fully devoted to YHWH as David's heart had been). (2) 'vehatov be'einekha asiti, and the good in your eyes I have done.' Then: 'vayivkeh Chizkiyahu bekhiyah gedolah, and Hezekiah wept with great weeping.' The weeping (bekhiyah gedolah = great weeping) is unreserved grief — not resignation but vigorous protest and appeal. The prayer argues covenant-faithfulness as the ground for divine response.
4: 'Then the word of the LORD came to Isaiah' — 'vayehi debar-YHWH el-Yesha'yahu, and the word of YHWH came to Isaiah.' The prophetic reception formula: the divine word 'comes to' the prophet (the standard formula for prophetic oracle reception appearing in Jer, Ezek, Hos, Joel, Jon, Mic, Zeph, Hag, Zech). The brevity of this verse emphasizes the immediacy of YHWH's response: the prayer (v3) is immediately followed by the divine word arriving at the prophet (v4), which is immediately followed by the oracle of healing (v5). The speed of response: Hezekiah wept, the word came to Isaiah, Isaiah came with new oracle. The narrative compresses the timeline to convey the urgency of divine response to prayer.
5: 'Go and tell Hezekiah: This is what the LORD says, the God of your father David: I have heard your prayer; I have seen your tears. I will add fifteen years to your life' — the divine reversal: 'halokh ve'amarta el-Chizkiyahu, go and say to Hezekiah.' The oracle identifies YHWH specifically as 'the God of your father David' (Elohei David avikha) = invoking the Davidic covenant as the basis for the healing, just as 37:35 grounded Jerusalem's defense 'for the sake of my servant David.' The three divine acknowledgments: 'samati et-tefillatekhah, I have heard your prayer; ra'iti et-dimatekhah, I have seen your tears; hinneni yosif al-yamekha chamesh esreh shanah, behold I am adding to your days fifteen years.' The divine 'seeing' of tears (dimatekhah) echoes the Exodus-event: 'I have indeed seen the misery of my people' (Exodus 3:7). The fifteen additional years are specific and measurable — the covenant God calculates human time.
6: 'I will rescue you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria, and I will defend this city' — the personal healing linked to the national defense: 'vehitsaltikhah ve'et ha'ir hazot mikkaf melek Ashshur, and I will rescue you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria.' The oracle connects Hezekiah's personal recovery to Jerusalem's protection — the two crises (personal illness and Assyrian siege) are addressed by the same divine word. The pattern: YHWH who adds 15 years to Hezekiah's life also defends Jerusalem for 15+ years. 'And I will defend this city' (vegeranoti al-ha'ir hazot) = the same formula as Isaiah 37:35 — the defense of Jerusalem is grounded in both Hezekiah's faithful prayer (Isaiah 38:3) and the Davidic covenant (Isaiah 37:35), with the personal and political inseparably linked.
7: 'And this will be the sign from the LORD to you that the LORD will carry out what he has said' — the sign offered to confirm the oracle: 'vezeh lekha ha'ot me'et YHWH, and this is the sign to you from YHWH.' The 'sign' (ot = a confirming indicator) = the same function as the signs of Isaiah 7:11-14 (Ahaz's refused sign and the Immanuel sign), Isaiah 8:18 (Isaiah's children as signs), and Isaiah 37:30 (the three-year agricultural recovery sign). In each case, the sign both confirms the prophetic word and makes the future event present in the current moment — the shadow reversing on the sundial is the present-tense miracle that guarantees the future promise of healing. YHWH offers signs to accommodate human faith-needs; the sign is not demanded but given freely.
8: 'I will make the shadow on the sundial of Ahaz — the shadow that has already gone down ten steps — go back ten steps. And the sun reversed itself on the steps by ten degrees' — the sundial sign: 'hinneni mashiv et-tsel hama'alot asher yareda bema'alot Achaz, behold I am returning the shadow of the steps that went down on the steps of Ahaz' — 'the sundial of Ahaz' (ma'alot Achaz = the steps/staircase of Ahaz) = a step-sundial or shadow-clock built by Hezekiah's father Ahaz. The shadow going backward (ten steps reversed) = a miraculous reversal of apparent solar movement. 2 Kings 20:9-11 specifies that Hezekiah was offered a choice: shadow going forward ten steps or backward ten steps; he chose backward as the greater miracle. Whether this was a local atmospheric refraction event, a miraculous suspension of natural law, or a symbolically presented miracle, the sign is clear: YHWH who controls the sun can reverse even time itself. Joshua 10:12-14 (the sun standing still at Gibeon) is the other great solar-control miracle.
9: 'The writing of Hezekiah king of Judah, composed after his illness, when he recovered' — the miktav superscription: 'michtav lechizqiyahu melek-Yehudah bachaloto vayechi mechlyayyto, the writing of Hezekiah king of Judah after his being sick, when he recovered from his sickness.' The michtav (written piece = a composition put in writing, a unique title in the OT psalter context; only here; related to kathav = to write). The autobiographical note: this is a royal composition, the only psalm in Isaiah attributed to a specific historical king. The date: 'after his illness, when he recovered' = a thanksgiving-retrospective composed from the other side of the near-death experience. The literary form is the individual thanksgiving psalm: description of distress, cry for help, divine response, vow of praise.
10: 'I said: In the prime of my life I must enter the gates of Sheol, deprived of the rest of my years' — the death-lament of Hezekiah's psalm: 'ani amarti biddemi yamai elekha beshaarei She'ol, I said: in the cutting off of my days I must go to the gates of Sheol.' The demi yamai (the cutting off of my days = the prime/middle of my life, from damam = to be cut off/silenced) — the death coming before the life is complete. 'The gates of Sheol' (sha'arei She'ol = the entrance to the underworld = the point of no return) — Sheol is the OT term for the abode of the dead (equivalent to Hades, the Akkadian Irkalla, the Ugaritic mt realm). 'Deprived of the rest of my years' (puqqadti yeter shenotay, I am deprived of the remainder of my years) — the years that should have remained are taken away prematurely. The lament is not about dying per se but about the incompleteness of death in midlife.
11: 'I said: I will not see the LORD again, even the LORD, in the land of the living; I will gaze on no human face again among those who dwell in the world' — the Sheol-theology of the OT: 'amarti lo-er'eh Yah Yah be'erets hachayim, I said: I will not see Yah, Yah, in the land of the living.' The land of the living (erets hachayim = the realm of the living, contrasted with Sheol) is the place of YHWH-encounter. The double Yah (the shortened divine name) expresses the intensity of the loss. The OT's developed understanding of afterlife is minimal in most pre-exilic texts: Sheol is the shadow-existence where YHWH is not worshiped and cannot be encountered (Psalms 6:5; Isaiah 88:11-12; Isaiah 115:17). The loss Hezekiah mourns is the loss of the worship-relationship with YHWH — not merely physical death but the ending of covenant fellowship. 'I will gaze on no human face again' (uvadam lo-abit od im-yoshevei chadal, and a human I will not look on among the inhabitants of cessation) = the breaking of all community.
12: 'My home has been pulled up and taken from me like a shepherd's tent; I have rolled up my life like a weaver; he cuts me from the loom. From day to night you bring me to an end' — the three weaving/tent metaphors for life's fragility: (1) 'nissa venistal minni ke'ohel ro'i dori, my dwelling has been pulled up and taken from me like a shepherd's tent' — the shepherd's tent that is packed up and moved instantly, leaving nothing behind. (2) 'qippadti kao'reg chayay, I have rolled up my life like a weaver' — life as a cloth being rolled up at the end of the day's weaving. (3) 'medallah yevazze'eni, he cuts me from the loom' — the finished cloth cut from the loom = death as the severing of the connection between the life-thread and its foundation. 'From day to night you bring me to an end' (miyyom ad-layelah tashlimeni, from day to night you finish me) = the rapid movement from morning vitality to night-death within a single day's span.
13: 'I held on through the night, but like a lion he was crushing all my bones. From day to night you bring me to an end' — the sleepless night of illness: 'shivviti ad-boqer kaarayeh ken yishaber kol-atsmotai, I kept composed until morning — like a lion he was crushing all my bones.' The night of severe illness as the lion's attack: the pain imagined as a great predator crushing bones. Bone-crushing by a lion = the imagery of extreme, physical, inescapable suffering (Job 30:17: 'the night pierces my bones; my gnawing pains never rest'; Psalms 22:14-15: 'I am poured out like water, all my bones are out of joint; my heart has turned to wax'). The repeat: 'from day to night you bring me to an end' (miyyom ad-layelah tashlimeni) = the same refrain as v12, creating a rhythmic urgency — each day threatens to be the last.
14: 'Like a swift or a crane, so did I chatter; I moaned like a dove; my eyes grew weary gazing upward. LORD, I am in distress — be my pledge of safety' — the bird-imagery of desperate prayer: 'kesus agur ken atsfatsef, like a swift, like a crane, so I chirped.' The tsaftsuf (to chirp = the thin, high-pitched cry of small birds, the antithesis of the deep, resonant prayer of the worship-assembly). The sick person's prayer reduced to bird-like chirping = the diminishment of voice that illness causes. 'I moaned like a dove' (ehgeh kayonah, I murmured like a dove = the low, throaty cooing of the dove, the sound of grief in ANE poetry, cf. Isaiah 59:11; Nahum 2:7). 'My eyes grew weary gazing upward' (dalleu einai lammarom, my eyes are languid/failing, gazing toward the heights) = physical weakness combined with continued prayer. 'LORD, I am in distress — be my pledge of safety' (YHWH ashaqah li, araveni, LORD I am oppressed; be surety for me) — araveni (be my pledge/surety = the term for a guarantor who takes responsibility for another's debt or safety).
15: 'What can I say? He spoke — and he himself did it. I will walk slowly all my years, weighted down by the bitterness of my soul' — the reversal of the lament into thanksgiving: 'mah-adabber ve'amar li vehu asah, what shall I say — and he said to me — and he himself did it.' The speechlessness of the healed: all the words of lamentation (vv10-14) are rendered insufficient by the divine act of healing. 'I will walk slowly all my years' (eddaddeh kol-shenotai, I will walk carefully/deliberately all my years) = the post-illness posture of deliberate, measured living rather than presuming on time. 'Weighted down by the bitterness of my soul' (al-mar nafshi, over the bitterness of my soul) = the ongoing awareness of having been near death — the bitterness of the illness-experience remains as a productive humility.
16: 'O Lord, by such things people live, and through all these things is the life of my spirit. So restore my health and let me live' — the theological reflection on suffering as life-giving: 'Adonai aleyhem yichyu ulkhol-bahen chayye-ruchi, O Lord, by them people live, and through all of them is the life of my spirit.' The 'such things' (aleyhem) = the things just described: the bitter suffering, the near-death experience, the wrestling in prayer — these are the very experiences through which life deepens. Suffering as pedagogy: not minimized ('it wasn't really that bad') but embraced ('through these very things the spirit lives'). 'So restore my health and let me live' (vetachlimeni vehachayeni, and you have healed me and revived me) — the transition from petition to thanksgiving as the healing is already accomplished in retrospect.
17: 'See — it was for my welfare that I suffered such bitter anguish; but you held my life back from the pit of destruction, and you have cast all my sins behind your back' — the theological interpretation of suffering: 'hineh leshalom mar-li mar, behold for my peace it was very bitter to me' — the paradox of the felix culpa: the bitterness (mar, the bitterness of Marah in Exodus 15:23) was for my shalom (peace/welfare). The suffering that produced the shalom is the divine pedagogy of Isaiah 26:16: 'when your discipline falls on them, they whispered their prayers.' 'You held my life back from the pit of destruction' (vattachshok nafshi mishachat, and you held back my soul from the pit) = the divine restraint that prevented the descent into She'ol. The climax: 'vekhishlaakhta acher gevekhah kol-chetai, and you have cast behind your back all my sins' — the complete forgiveness expressed as the most emphatic possible distance: thrown behind YHWH's back, out of his sight forever (cf. Micah 7:19: 'you will hurl all our iniquities into the depths of the sea').
18: 'For Sheol cannot thank you; death cannot praise you; those who descend to the pit cannot hope in your faithfulness' — the OT pneumatology of Sheol: 'ki lo She'ol todekha, for Sheol cannot thank you.' The three negatives: Sheol cannot give thanks (yadah = the thanksgiving of the worship-assembly), death cannot praise (halal = the hallelujah-praise), those in the pit cannot hope in YHWH's faithfulness (emeth = covenant-faithfulness). This is the pre-resurrection theology of most of the OT: the dead in Sheol are cut off from active worship (Psalms 6:5; Isaiah 30:9; Isaiah 88:10-12; Isaiah 115:17). The theological urgency: if only the living can praise, then preserving life is preserving praise. Hezekiah's prayer for life is simultaneously a prayer for continued worship. The NT contrast is stark: Revelation 5:13 has 'every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth' praising — the resurrection breaks the Sheol-silence.
19: 'Only the living, the living, give you thanks — as I do today. A father makes known your faithfulness to his children' — the priority of living praise: 'chay chay hu yodekha kamooni hayyom, the living, the living — he gives thanks to you, as I do today.' The emphatic repetition (chay chay = living, living = double emphasis on the vitality required for true praise). 'As I do today' (kamooni hayyom) = the praise happening in the present moment of Hezekiah's recovered health is the fulfillment of the argument: the living man praises. 'A father makes known your faithfulness to his children' (av el-banim yodi'a el-amittekha, a father to children makes known your faithfulness) = the inter-generational transmission of covenant testimony. The healed person becomes the witness who declares YHWH's faithfulness to the next generation — the living praise extends through time.
20: 'The LORD was ready to save me. And we will play my songs of praise to stringed instruments all the days of our life in the house of the LORD' — the resolution in communal worship: 'YHWH lehoshi'eni, the LORD was ready/resolved to save me.' The lahoshi'eni (to save me = the infinitive construct expressing YHWH's determined purpose) = YHWH's saving was not reluctant or uncertain but resolved. 'And we will play my songs of praise to stringed instruments all the days of our life in the house of the LORD' (unaginnotai nenaggein kol-yemei chayyenu al-beit YHWH, and my songs of praise we will play on stringed instruments all the days of our life in the house of YHWH). The singular ('my songs') becomes plural ('we will play') = the personal thanksgiving becoming communal worship. The 'house of the LORD' (beit YHWH) = the temple as the permanent site of the ongoing praise that Sheol cannot give but the living community can.
21: 'Now Isaiah had said, Let them take a pressed cake of figs and apply it as a poultice on the sore, and he will recover' — the medical treatment: 'vayomer Yesha'yahu yisse'u deveylat te'enim, and Isaiah had said: let them take a lump of pressed figs.' The deveilat te'enim (a pressed-fig cake/poultice) = figs pressed into a mass, used as a poultice on skin infections and wounds. Fig poultices had known medicinal uses in the ANE — the warm, moist application was thought to draw out infection (modern research suggests figs have some anti-inflammatory properties). 'And apply it on the sore, and he will recover' (veyimrach al-hashchin veichyeh) — the 'sore' (shchin = an inflamed/festering sore, the same word as the Egyptian boils of Exodus 9:9-10; Job 2:7). The theological point: YHWH's healing works through the natural medicine provided. This is the pattern throughout OT healing: 2 Kings 5:10 (Elisha tells Naaman to wash in the Jordan); John 9:6 (Jesus makes mud for the blind man's eyes) — divine healing and natural means working together.
22: 'And Hezekiah had asked, What will be the sign that I shall go up to the house of the LORD?' — the sign-request for temple access: 'vayomer Chizkiyahu mah ot ki e'aleh beit YHWH, and Hezekiah said: what is the sign that I shall go up to the house of YHWH?' The request is for assurance that the healing will be complete enough to allow pilgrimage to the temple — the temple-visit as the marker of restored covenant fellowship. The question appears here as an appendix (2 Kgs 20:8 places it earlier in the narrative) — Isaiah provides the sundial sign (v8) as the answer to this request for a sign. The temple-visit as recovery-goal: Hezekiah's concern is not merely physical health but restoration of the worship-relationship with YHWH that illness had interrupted.
